title: Debugging & Instruments
description: Master Xcode's debugging tools and Instruments profiler — set breakpoints, inspect variables, use LLDB commands, and diagnose performance issues with Instruments templates.

sections.content:
## Debugging with Xcode

Xcode's debugger is built on LLDB (Low Level Debugger) and integrates directly into the IDE. When your app hits a breakpoint, execution pauses and you can inspect the current state of your program.

### Breakpoints

Click the gutter next to a line number to set a breakpoint (blue arrow). Right-click a breakpoint to edit conditions — you can pause execution only when a variable equals a specific value or after a hit count threshold is reached. Exception breakpoints are especially useful: they pause whenever an error is thrown, showing you the exact line that caused the crash.

### The Debug Bar

When paused, the debug bar at the top of the debug area provides controls:

- **Continue** (Cmd+Ctrl+Y) — Resume execution
- **Step Over** (F6) — Execute the current line and move to the next
- **Step Into** (F7) — Enter a function call
- **Step Out** (F8) — Finish the current function and return to the caller

### LLDB Console Commands

Type directly into the LLDB console in the debug area:

- `po variableName` — Print object description
- `frame variable` — Show all local variables
- `expr someExpression` — Evaluate an expression at runtime
- `thread backtrace` — Show the call stack

sections.title: Debugging a Logic Error

sections.content:
## Instruments

Instruments is a performance analysis and testing tool bundled with Xcode. Access it via **Product > Profile** (Cmd+I). Instruments provides dozens of templates for profiling different aspects of your app:

### Key Instruments Templates

- **Time Profiler** — Identify which functions consume the most CPU time. Look for unexpected spikes or functions called too frequently.
- **Allocations** — Track object creation and destruction. Useful for finding memory leaks where objects are never deallocated.
- **Leaks** — Automatically detect retain cycles and leaked memory that your app no longer references but hasn't freed.
- **Energy Log** — Measure energy impact including CPU, network, and location usage, which is critical for battery life on mobile devices.

### Workflow Tips

1. Always profile on a real device — the simulator doesn't accurately reflect performance.
2. Use the call tree view and check **Invert Call Tree** to see which methods at the top of the stack consume the most time.
3. Set recording flags to mark specific points during a profiling session for later comparison.

sections.title: Simulating a Performance Bottleneck

sections.question: Which LLDB command prints the object description of a variable in the debug console?
sections.explanation: The 'po' command (print object) in LLDB prints the debug description of an object, which is the standard way to inspect variables at runtime.

sections.question: Which Instruments template is best for finding retain cycles and leaked memory?
sections.explanation: The Leaks instrument specifically detects memory that has been allocated but is no longer reachable — a clear sign of retain cycles or orphaned references.
